text: Hello everybody, I am Dr. Senthil Prabhu from Tirupur. This is a video of laparoscopic appendicectomy for appendicular perforation.
This is the panoramic view.
The laparoscopic view shows the cecum and appendix adhered to the right parietal wall, with the appendix visibly stuck to the wall.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix, which appears healthy. The continuous line represents the appendix, while the dotted line likely indicates the cecum and ascending colon.
The surgeon meticulously coagulates and cuts the peritoneum over the appendix, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
Whenever the anatomy is different or you feel it is a difficult surgery, it is better to proceed in small steps.
The surgeon divides the fibers surrounding the appendix in a meticulous fiber-by-fiber manner, aiding in the clear delineation of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon strips the peritoneum away from the appendix, tracing the appendix from its base towards the tip, revealing the anatomical structure beneath.

The surgeon exposes the peritoneum covering the tip of the appendix, revealing the course of the appendix. The mid part of the appendix appears distended, suggesting the presence of a fecolith or an abscess.
The surgeon identifies the tip of the appendix located in the subhepatic position, revealing its anatomical orientation.
The surgeon performs a retrograde appendectomy due to the unclear visibility of the appendix tip, dividing the mesoappendix near the base of the appendix using a bipolar cautery device.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the fluid field, revealing the mesoappendix. The appendix is then divided at a point well away from the cecum to clearly define its base.
Saline lavage is applied to clear blood from the surgical field, enhancing visibility. The surgeon divides the mesoappendix, preparing to identify and address the base of the appendix later.
The surgeon performs a submucosal appendectomy, revealing the site of perforation with a small amount of abscess. The mucosa of the appendix is visible.
The surgeon performs a retrograde appendectomy, gradually stripping the mucosa from the underlying muscle during submucosal dissection.

The surgeon applies progressive traction and counter traction to divide the serosa, successfully removing the appendix in a retrograde manner. The dissection proceeds until the tip of the appendix is fully separated.
The surgeon applies gentle traction and uses energy devices to dissect the appendix, progressing towards the tip of the structure.
The surgeon uses irrigation and saline lavage along with continuous traction to separate the appendix and mesoappendix from the cecum and ascending colon, revealing the tip of the appendix.
The surgeon releases the attachments at the base of the appendix, revealing a 1.5 centimeter stump. A catgut loop is prepared to secure the stump.
Two knots are applied close to the base of the appendix, securing it before the remaining stump is excised.
The surgeon converts the right hand working port into a titanium port for specimen extraction. The appendix specimen is extracted, followed by thorough saline lavage of the surgical area.
